    Home › Types › Trijets

    Trijets

    Three engines: the answer to a rule that no longer exists.

    Aircraft here4
    Longest61.62 m
    Combined built2,728
    First flights1963–1990
    What it means

    The category, explained

    The trijet existed because of regulation. Before modern ETOPS rules, twin-engine aircraft were barred from flying far from a diversion airport, so an aircraft crossing an ocean needed three engines or four. Three was cheaper than four — hence the DC-10, the L-1011 and later the MD-11, each with an engine buried in the tail.

    Once twins were certified for long overwater flights, the economics collapsed instantly: two engines burn less fuel than three. No trijet airliner has been built since 2000, and the last freighters are now retiring.

    The aircraft

    Every trijet here

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
